title: What Is Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?
content:
When a development project raises questions about whether a site has significant historical or cultural value, federal and state regulations require a formal assessment before construction can proceed. Phase II archaeological evaluation is the critical middle step in that process—bridging the preliminary survey and, when necessary, full data recovery excavation. Understanding how it works helps agencies, developers, and project planners avoid costly delays and stay in compliance with federal preservation law.

Quick Answer: Phase II archaeological evaluation is a targeted excavation study that tests a site identified during Phase I survey to determine whether it meets the National Register of Historic Places (NRHP) eligibility criteria. It answers the question: is this site significant enough to require avoidance or further investigation before a project can proceed?

What Triggers a Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?

A Phase II evaluation is triggered when a Phase I survey identifies a site that may be eligible for the National Register of Historic Places, but more data is needed to make a definitive determination. At that point, regulators and consulting parties—typically a State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO) or Tribal Historic Preservation Office (THPO)—require additional testing before the project can advance.

Common triggers include the discovery of dense artifact concentrations, intact subsurface features such as hearths, pits, or structural remains, or evidence suggesting a site's historical associations may be significant. Projects governed by Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA) are the most frequent context, but state-level review processes and environmental impact assessments can require Phase II work as well.

Federal undertakings—transportation projects, utility corridors, military installations, flood control infrastructure—are among the most common project types that reach the Phase II stage. Private developers whose projects involve federal permits, licenses, or funding are equally subject to these requirements.

How Is a Phase II Evaluation Different from Phase I?

Phase I survey is a broad reconnaissance effort designed to identify whether archaeological sites are present within a project area. Phase II evaluation, by contrast, is intensive and targeted—it focuses on a specific site to determine its significance and integrity. The shift is from discovery to assessment.

Where Phase I fieldwork typically involves surface inspection and widely spaced shovel tests across large tracts of land, Phase II excavation concentrates effort on a defined site boundary. Archaeologists open larger block excavations or closely spaced test units to expose subsurface deposits, features, and artifact patterns. The density and distribution of evidence reveal how intact the site is and whether it retains the information needed to address important research questions.

Data collected during Phase II informs a formal NRHP eligibility determination—a conclusion reached jointly with SHPO, THPO, and often the federal lead agency. This determination shapes every subsequent decision about the project's design, routing, or mitigation requirements.

What Methods Do Archaeologists Use in Phase II Fieldwork?

Phase II fieldwork combines systematic excavation with a suite of scientific analyses designed to extract maximum information from the site. The specific methods depend on site type, deposit depth, and the research questions being addressed.

Standard fieldwork approaches include:

  • Block excavations – contiguous 1×1 or 1×2 meter units opened to expose horizontal patterns across features or activity areas
  • Trenching – mechanical or hand-dug trenches used to assess deposit depth, stratigraphy, and feature extent across larger areas
  • Feature documentation – detailed mapping, photography, and sampling of intact features such as postholes, hearths, trash pits, and burials
  • Soil flotation – recovery of botanical remains, seeds, and small faunal bone from feature fill
  • Radiocarbon and other absolute dating – establishing a reliable chronology for site occupation

GIS and remote sensing tools are increasingly integrated into Phase II projects, allowing archaeologists to model subsurface conditions, refine excavation strategies, and produce precise site maps that support the eligibility report.

What Are the Criteria for NRHP Eligibility?

To recommend a site as eligible for the National Register of Historic Places, archaeologists must demonstrate that it meets at least one of four criteria established by the National Park Service. Most archaeological sites are evaluated under Criterion D—the site has yielded or may yield information important to history or prehistory.

A site qualifies under Criterion D when its intact deposits contain data capable of answering significant research questions about past human behavior, settlement patterns, subsistence strategies, technological change, or social organization. The key considerations are:

  • Integrity – are the deposits sufficiently undisturbed to retain their original context and associations?
  • Data potential – do the types of artifacts and features present have the capacity to address meaningful research questions?
  • Research significance – do those questions matter to the broader understanding of regional or national history?

Sites may also be evaluated under Criteria A, B, or C if they are associated with important historical events, significant persons, or distinctive patterns of architecture and design. Archaeological investigations at historic-period sites frequently address multiple criteria simultaneously.

What Happens After the Phase II Report Is Submitted?

The Phase II investigation culminates in a technical report submitted to the lead agency and SHPO or THPO for review. The report presents the fieldwork methods, artifact and feature data, site chronology, and a formal recommendation regarding NRHP eligibility. Consulting parties then reach a consensus determination—eligible, not eligible, or needs further study.

If the site is determined not eligible, no further archaeological work is required and the project can proceed in that area. If the site is determined eligible, the project team must either redesign the project to avoid the site entirely or, where avoidance is not feasible, negotiate a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) that typically requires Phase III data recovery—full-scale excavation to retrieve the site's significant data before construction disturbs it.

Timely, well-documented Phase II work reduces the risk of last-minute project redesigns. Engaging an experienced cultural resource management firm early ensures the evaluation meets agency and SHPO standards the first time, keeping projects on schedule.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does a Phase II archaeological evaluation take?

Timeline varies by site size and complexity, but most Phase II projects require two to six weeks of fieldwork followed by four to twelve weeks of laboratory analysis, reporting, and agency review. Large or deeply stratified sites, or those involving bioarchaeological remains, can extend the process considerably.

Who pays for Phase II archaeological evaluation?

Under Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act, the project proponent—typically the developer, transportation agency, or permit applicant—is responsible for funding all phases of archaeological investigation, including Phase II evaluation. Federal agencies may fund investigations on federally owned or managed lands.

Can a project proceed if a site is found eligible after Phase II?

Yes. Eligibility does not automatically stop a project. The lead agency, SHPO, THPO, and applicant must consult to resolve adverse effects, usually through avoidance, redesign, or a Memorandum of Agreement requiring Phase III data recovery before ground-disturbing construction occurs.

What qualifications are required to conduct Phase II evaluations?

Archaeologists leading Phase II evaluations must meet the Secretary of the Interior's Professional Qualification Standards, typically requiring a graduate degree in archaeology or anthropology and supervised field experience. Many states and federal agencies additionally require RPA (Register of Professional Archaeologists) certification or equivalent credentials.

Is Phase II required for every archaeological site found during Phase I?

No. Phase II is only required when a Phase I survey identifies a site with sufficient evidence to suggest it may meet NRHP eligibility criteria. Sites that clearly lack integrity or data potential can be recommended not eligible at Phase I, bypassing the need for further evaluation.

Plan Your Phase II Evaluation with Confidence

Phase II archaeological evaluation is one of the most consequential steps in the Section 106 compliance process. A rigorous, well-documented evaluation protects project schedules by resolving eligibility questions definitively—and positions your project for a smooth path through agency review. Whether you are managing a highway corridor, a utility right-of-way, or a large-scale development, working with qualified archaeologists who know the regional regulatory landscape makes all the difference.

excerpt: Phase II archaeological evaluation tests sites found during Phase I survey to determine National Register eligibility under Section 106 of the NHPA.
